\begin{keyidea}{How to read this document}
This document explains exactly what is implemented in the \file{agent-runtime}
project folder. Every claim traces to a specific file, and where a claim could be
checked by running the code, it was run: the test suite, the command line tool, the
scenario suite and the replay path were all executed while writing this, and what
they printed is reported here rather than assumed.

Technical terms are defined the first time they appear, in a boxed callout. Assume
no prior knowledge: if you have never met event sourcing or deterministic replay,
Sections 2 and 9 build both from nothing.

Red boxes are honest findings. Some repeat limitations the project's own README
already states; others are defects found by reading and running the code that the
README does not mention. The two are labelled differently, because the second kind
is the kind an interviewer might find first.
\end{keyidea}

\tableofcontents
\newpage

% ============================================================
\section{What this project is}
% ============================================================

\textbf{agent-runtime} is a Python library and command line tool for running
\emph{LLM agents} with the operational machinery that a bare ``call the model, run a
tool, repeat'' loop leaves out: a durable record of every run, the ability to replay
a run exactly, recovery from a crash mid-run, a human approval step before anything
with a side effect happens, deliberately injected failures to prove the system
degrades safely, and a suite of scripted regression tests that catch behavior changes
when the underlying model changes.

\begin{jargon}{LLM agent}
A \emph{large language model} (LLM) is a machine learning model that produces text
from text. An \emph{LLM agent} is a program that asks such a model, in a loop, ``given
what has happened so far, what should happen next?'' The model's answer is either a
final text answer, or a request to call one or more \emph{tools}: functions the
program exposes, such as ``read this file'' or ``send this HTTP request''. The program
executes the requested tools, feeds the results back to the model, and asks again.
This project implements exactly that loop in \file{src/agent\_runtime/runtime.py}.
\end{jargon}

\subsection{Why it exists, in the project's own framing}

The README states the thesis directly: the agent loop itself is a small amount of
code, but what makes an agent \emph{deployable} is everything around it. Knowing
exactly what happened in a specific run. Being able to reproduce that run. Knowing
whether the agent emailed a customer before a human approved it. Knowing whether a new
model version still passes the flows that used to work.

The design response to all of this is a single idea, applied consistently:
\textbf{every run is an append-only log of events, and every feature is a function
computed from that log.}

% ============================================================
\section{Event sourcing, from first principles}
% ============================================================

Almost every program you have used stores \emph{current state}. A spreadsheet holds
the value in each cell. When you change a cell, the old value is overwritten and gone.
This is simple and it is what most software does.

Event sourcing inverts that.

\begin{jargon}{Event sourcing}
A design pattern where, instead of storing ``the current state'' of something and
overwriting it as things change, you store every state-changing \emph{event} as an
immutable, ordered record, and compute ``current state'' on demand by replaying those
events from the start. The log never loses information, so you can always recompute
the past, reproduce it, or audit it.
\end{jargon}

A bank account is the classic illustration. You could store one number, the balance,
and overwrite it on every transaction. Or you could store the list of deposits and
withdrawals, and compute the balance by adding them up. The second is slower to read
and it uses more disk, and in exchange it can answer questions the first can never
answer: what was my balance on the 3rd? Why is it this number? Which transaction was
wrong? Undo that one thing without losing the rest.

\begin{keyidea}{Why an AI agent in particular wants this}
An agent's problems are exactly the problems the log solves.

An agent is \emph{nondeterministic}: ask the same model the same question twice and
you may get different answers. It is \emph{expensive}: every step costs money and
seconds. It \emph{acts on the world}: it writes files and drafts emails, and those
actions do not un-happen. And it is \emph{opaque}: when it does something strange, the
only honest answer to ``why did it do that?'' is a complete record of what it saw and
what it chose.

Store only current state and you have none of that. You have a finished run, a
changed workspace, and no way to answer any question about how you got there. Store
the log and ``what happened'', ``do it again exactly'', ``continue from the crash'' and
``did the new model change behavior'' all become the same kind of question: a function
over a list of events.
\end{keyidea}

The rest of this document is, in a real sense, that one idea and its consequences.
Every feature in Sections 7 through 15 is a function over the log.

% ============================================================
\section{Architecture at a glance}
% ============================================================

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=1.05cm and 1.05cm]

  % Lay the three collaborators out from the centre one, so they cannot overlap.
  \node[box, below=1.6cm of rt] (exec) {ToolExecutor\\+ ToolRegistry};
  \node[box, left=0.9cm of exec] (adapter)
    {ModelAdapter\\mock / anthropic\\/ openai};
  \node[box, right=0.9cm of exec] (appr) {Approval policy\\auto / human};

  \node[store, below=3.7cm of rt, minimum width=5cm] (st)
    {\textbf{EventStore} (SQLite)\\append-only \code{events} table\\+ idempotency ledger};

  \node[box, right=2.2cm of st, fill=white, draw=okgreen] (proj)
    {Projection\\log $\rightarrow$ RunState\\\code{projection.py}};

  \draw[flow] (cli) -- (rt);
  \draw[flow] (web) -- (rt);
  \draw[flow] (scen) -- (rt);
  \draw[flow] (rt) -- (adapter);
  \draw[flow] (rt) -- (exec);
  \draw[flow] (rt) -- (appr);
  \draw[flow] (adapter) -- (st);
  \draw[flow] (exec) -- (st);
  \draw[flow] (appr) -- (st);
  % The runtime appends directly too. Route this around the left, so it does not
  % strike through the ToolExecutor box on its way down.
  \draw[flow] (rt.west) to[out=180, in=90] ($(adapter.north west)+(-0.8,0.25)$)
    to[out=270, in=180] (st.west);
  \draw[dflow] (st) -- (proj);
  \draw[dflow] (proj.north) to[out=90,in=0] node[lbl,pos=0.6]{every decision\\re-derived} (rt.east);
\end{tikzpicture}
}
\caption{Solid arrows are appends; dashed arrows are reads through the projection.
Nothing reads the store except through \code{project()}.}
\end{figure}

Every arrow into the SQLite store is an \emph{append}. The codebase contains no UPDATE
and no DELETE against the \code{events} table: the schema and every SQL statement in
\file{store.py} is an \code{INSERT} or a \code{SELECT}, confirmed by reading that file
in full. Every arrow out of the store goes through \file{projection.py}, the only
module that interprets what a sequence of events means. The CLI, the web UI, the
replay comparator and the scenario assertions all call the same \code{project()}
function, so they cannot disagree about the state of a run.

\begin{jargon}{SQLite}
A small relational database engine that needs no separate server process: the whole
database is one ordinary file on disk. This project uses it as the single persistent
store for every run's event log, and separately as the storage behind the example
calendar tool.
\end{jargon}

% ============================================================
\section{The event log}
% ============================================================

\subsection{The table}

From \file{store.py}, the schema actually created is:

\begin{lstlisting}[language=SQL]
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events (
    run_id  TEXT    NOT NULL,
    seq     INTEGER NOT NULL,
    type    TEXT    NOT NULL,
    ts      REAL    NOT NULL,
    payload TEXT    NOT NULL,   -- a JSON blob
    PRIMARY KEY (run_id, seq)
);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_events_type ON events (run_id, type);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS executions (      -- the idempotency ledger, Sec. 8
    idem_key TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    run_id   TEXT NOT NULL,
    result   TEXT NOT NULL,
    ts       REAL NOT NULL
);
\end{lstlisting}

\begin{jargon}{Primary key, and what append-only means here}
A \emph{primary key} is a column or combination of columns that must be unique across
rows. Here it is \code{(run\_id, seq)}: within one run, each sequence number can be
claimed exactly once. \code{EventStore.append()} reads \code{MAX(seq)+1} for the run,
tries to insert, and retries (up to 5 times) if another writer claimed that number
first. SQLite's uniqueness constraint rejects the loser, who re-reads the new maximum
and tries again. That is how two processes can append to one run safely without a
lock.
\end{jargon}

\subsection{The twelve event types}

\file{events.py} defines twelve event type constants. Grouped in the order a typical
successful run emits them:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{4.1cm}p{9.6cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Event type} & \textbf{Payload highlights} \\
\midrule
\code{run\_created} & agent name, the user's request text, and free-form \code{meta}: workspace path, which adapter, scenario file and behavior version if any \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{model\_requested} & a call number, plus a \emph{digest} and count of the messages about to be sent, not the messages themselves \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{model\_responded} & the parsed turn (text and/or tool calls), token usage, model name; or \code{malformed: true} with the raw unparseable text \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{model\_error} & a transport or provider failure for one attempt, such as an HTTP 500 \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{tool\_requested} & call id, tool name, arguments, and whether the tool is side-effecting \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{tool\_approved}\newline \code{tool\_denied} & who decided, and the deny reason if any \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{tool\_executed}\newline \code{tool\_failed} & the tool's result or error, the attempt count, and a \code{replayed} flag \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{checkpoint} & how many model calls and resolved tool calls have happened so far \\
\addlinespace[2pt]
\code{run\_finished}\newline \code{run\_failed} & the model's final answer text, or a machine-readable failure cause \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\begin{jargon}{Digest, or hash}
A short fixed-length string computed from a larger piece of data, such that the same
input always gives the same digest and different inputs almost never collide. This
project uses SHA-256 truncated to 16 hex characters purely as a fingerprint of the
outgoing message list (\code{runtime.\_digest}). It is not a secret, just a compact
``was this the same request'' marker. Storing the digest rather than the messages
keeps the log small, and the messages are recoverable anyway: they are a pure function
of the log (Section 6).
\end{jargon}

\subsection{A real log, end to end}

This is not a sketch. It is the event sequence produced by actually running
\code{agentrun run --scenario scenarios/ops\_assistant/chase\_overdue\_acme.yaml},
approving the gated call, and reading the log back with \code{agentrun runs show}.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\resizebox{0.94\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=0.34cm and 0,
  ev/.style={rectangle, draw=inkblue, fill=softgrey, text=inkblue, align=left,
             font=\scriptsize\ttfamily, minimum width=13cm, minimum height=0.5cm,
             inner sep=4pt, rounded corners=2pt},
  gate/.style={ev, fill=white, draw=warnred, text=warnred},
  fin/.style={ev, fill=white, draw=okgreen, text=okgreen}]
  \node[ev] (e0)  {seq 0 \ \ run\_created \ \ \ \ \ agent=ops\_assistant, request="Chase the overdue invoice..."};
  \node[ev, below=of e0]  (e1)  {seq 1 \ \ model\_requested \ \ call\_number=1, messages\_digest=..., message\_count=2};
  \node[ev, below=of e1]  (e2)  {seq 2 \ \ model\_responded \ \ tool\_calls=[read\_file(invoices.csv)], usage=\{simulated: true\}};
  \node[ev, below=of e2]  (e3)  {seq 3 \ \ tool\_requested \ \ call\_id=call-1-0, tool=read\_file, side\_effect=false};
  \node[ev, below=of e3]  (e4)  {seq 4 \ \ tool\_executed \ \ \ result=\{invoice rows\}, attempts=1, replayed=false};
  \node[ev, below=of e4]  (e5)  {seq 5 \ \ checkpoint \ \ \ \ \ \ model\_calls=1, calls\_resolved=1};
  \node[ev, below=of e5]  (e6)  {seq 6 \ \ model\_requested \ \ call\_number=2};
  \node[ev, below=of e6]  (e7)  {seq 7 \ \ model\_responded \ \ tool\_calls=[draft\_email(to=billing@acme.example, ...)]};
  \node[gate, below=of e7](e8)  {seq 8 \ \ tool\_requested \ \ call\_id=call-2-0, tool=draft\_email, side\_effect=TRUE};
  \node[gate, below=of e8](e9)  {seq 9 \ \ tool\_approved \ \ \ call\_id=call-2-0, approver=cli \ \ <- the gate (Sec. 7)};
  \node[ev, below=of e9]  (e10) {seq 10 \ tool\_executed \ \ \ result=\{draft\_path: outbox/Overdue\_invoice\_INV-1001.eml\}};
  \node[ev, below=of e10] (e11) {seq 11 \ checkpoint \ \ \ \ \ \ model\_calls=2, calls\_resolved=2};
  \node[ev, below=of e11] (e12) {seq 12 \ model\_requested \ \ call\_number=3};
  \node[ev, below=of e12] (e13) {seq 13 \ model\_responded \ \ final text, no tool calls};
  \node[fin, below=of e13](e14) {seq 14 \ run\_finished \ \ \ \ final\_answer="Drafted a reminder email to Acme Corp..."};

  \draw[decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=5pt}, draw=warnred, thick]
    ($(e9.north east)+(0.2,0.05)$) -- ($(e8.south east)+(0.2,-0.05)$)
    node[midway, right=7pt, align=left, font=\scriptsize\itshape, text=warnred]
    {the run stopped here\\until a human approved};
\end{tikzpicture}
}
\caption{The 15 events of run \code{5eae35be7bc7}, verified by running it. Without an
auto-approve policy the loop returns \code{waiting\_approval} after seq 8, and seq 9
is written later by \code{agentrun approvals approve}. Events 10 to 14 are identical
either way, because they are computed from the log, not from which code path produced
the approval.}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Canonical encoding, for exact comparison}

Every \code{Event} has a \code{canonical()} method that JSON-encodes it with sorted
keys and no whitespace:

\begin{lstlisting}[language=Python]
json.dumps(body, sort_keys=True, separators=(",", ":"))
\end{lstlisting}

Two events that mean the same thing always produce the exact same string. This is what
lets replay (Section 9) claim ``byte-identical'' rather than ``looks about right''.
Note what \code{canonical()} excludes by default: the \code{run\_id}. A replayed run
has a different id by construction, so comparing logs would fail immediately if the id
were included. Everything else, including the timestamp, must match exactly.

% ============================================================
\section{The loop: \code{Runtime.drive()}}
% ============================================================

\file{runtime.py} is the heart of the project, and \code{drive(run\_id)} is, in the
project's own words, ``a fixed point iteration over the log''.

\begin{jargon}{Fixed point iteration}
A loop that repeatedly applies the same rule to its own output until the output stops
changing or a stated exit condition is hit. Here the rule is: derive the current state
from the log, decide the single next thing to do, append it, repeat. It is applied
identically whether this is the first iteration of a brand new run, the continuation
of a run a human just approved, or the resumption of a run that crashed a minute ago.
There is one code path, not three.
\end{jargon}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\resizebox{0.86\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=0.6cm and 1.6cm]
  \node[box, minimum width=5cm] (start) {project the log $\rightarrow$ RunState};
  \node[dec, below=of start] (terminal) {status\\terminal?};
  \node[box, fill=white, draw=okgreen, right=of terminal] (stop1) {return: run is done};
  \node[box, minimum width=5cm, below=of terminal] (resolve)
    {resolve unfinished tool calls\\in request order (Sec. 7)};
  \node[dec, below=of resolve] (paused) {side-effecting call\\undecided?};
  \node[box, fill=white, draw=warnred, right=of paused] (stop2)
    {return:\\\code{waiting\_approval}};
  \node[dec, below=of paused] (limit) {model-call\\limit hit?};
  \node[box, fill=white, draw=warnred, right=of limit] (stop3)
    {append \code{run\_failed}\\cause=\code{model\_call\_limit}};
  \node[box, minimum width=5cm, below=of limit] (checkpoint) {append \code{checkpoint}};
  \node[box, minimum width=5cm, below=of checkpoint] (model)
    {append \code{model\_requested},\\ask the adapter, append the outcome};
  \node[dec, below=of model] (final) {final answer?};
  \node[box, fill=white, draw=okgreen, right=of final] (stop4)
    {append \code{run\_finished},\\return};

  \draw[flow] (start) -- (terminal);
  \draw[flow] (terminal) -- node[lbl]{yes} (stop1);
  \draw[flow] (terminal) -- node[lbl]{no} (resolve);
  \draw[flow] (resolve) -- (paused);
  \draw[flow] (paused) -- node[lbl]{yes} (stop2);
  \draw[flow] (paused) -- node[lbl]{no} (limit);
  \draw[flow] (limit) -- node[lbl]{yes} (stop3);
  \draw[flow] (limit) -- node[lbl]{no} (checkpoint);
  \draw[flow] (checkpoint) -- (model);
  \draw[flow] (model) -- (final);
  \draw[flow] (final) -- node[lbl]{yes} (stop4);
  \draw[flow] (final.west) -- node[lbl, pos=0.75]{no: loop} ($(final.west)+(-1.5,0)$)
    |- (start.west);
\end{tikzpicture}
}
\caption{\code{Runtime.drive()}. Every exit is an append; every entry re-reads the log.}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Asking the model}

\code{\_model\_step()} rebuilds the conversation from the log via
\code{build\_messages()} (Section 6), appends \code{model\_requested}, then calls
\code{adapter.complete(messages, tool\_specs)}. Three outcomes are handled:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Transport or provider failure} (\code{AdapterError}): append
  \code{model\_error} and, if retries remain (default 2), sleep
  $0.5 \times 2^{\mathrm{attempt}}$ seconds before retrying. If retries are exhausted,
  append \code{run\_failed} with cause \code{adapter\_error: retries exhausted}.
  \item \textbf{Unparseable output} (\code{MalformedOutputError}): append
  \code{model\_responded} with \code{malformed: true} and the raw text, truncated to
  2000 characters. If this is more than \code{max\_malformed} (default 2) malformed
  responses in the run, fail the run with cause \code{malformed\_model\_output}.
  Otherwise return, so the next iteration feeds a corrective message back.
  \item \textbf{A parsed turn}: append \code{model\_responded}. If the turn has no tool
  calls (\code{ModelTurn.is\_final}), it is the final answer and \code{run\_finished}
  is appended. Otherwise each requested tool call becomes a \code{tool\_requested}
  event, tagged with whether that tool is side-effecting, looked up from the registry.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{jargon}{Exponential backoff}
A retry strategy where each successive retry waits longer than the last (here
doubling: 0.5s, then 1s, then 2s), so a struggling remote service is not immediately
hammered again at full speed.
\end{jargon}

\subsection{Checkpoints}

Before each model call except the first, the loop appends a \code{checkpoint}
recording how many model calls and resolved tool calls have happened. Read literally,
a checkpoint is \emph{not} required for correctness: every number in it is already
recomputable from the rest of the log. It is a cheap progress marker for a human
skimming \code{agentrun runs show}.

\begin{honest}{The checkpoint is a missed opportunity, and the README says so obliquely}
The README's own ``what I'd do differently'' says resume should record ``the adapter
cursor in a checkpoint payload instead of inferring it''. The \code{checkpoint} event
is already being written on every iteration and already carries two counters that are
pure functions of the log, which is to say it carries nothing that is not already
known. The one number that is \emph{not} recoverable from the log, the mock adapter's
script position, is the one it does not record. Section 18 shows this is not
hypothetical: it is a live off-by-one bug.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{Rebuilding the conversation from the log}
% ============================================================

\code{projection.build\_messages(state, system\_prompt)} turns the log into the
message list a model API expects. It walks the log in order and translates:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{4.4cm}p{9.3cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Log event} & \textbf{Message produced} \\
\midrule
\code{model\_responded} (normal) & an \code{assistant} message: the turn's text, plus \code{tool\_calls} if any \\
\code{model\_responded} (malformed) & a \code{user} message: ``Your previous output could not be parsed. Reply with either tool calls or a final answer.'' This is the corrective nudge from Section 5.2 \\
\code{tool\_executed} & a \code{tool} message carrying the result \\
\code{tool\_failed} & a \code{tool} message: \code{\{"error": ...\}} \\
\code{tool\_denied} & a \code{tool} message: \code{\{"denied": true, "reason": ...\}} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The last row is worth pausing on. It is literally how ``the operator said no'' becomes
something the model can react to. A denial is not an exception and not a stop; it is an
observation, in the same channel a tool result would arrive on. The model reads it and
chooses what to do next, exactly as it would after any other tool outcome.

Because this is a pure function of the log, a run resumed after a crash sends the model
exactly the messages an uninterrupted run would have sent. There is no separate
``resume state'' to keep in sync, because there is no state at all.

% ============================================================
\section{The approval gate}
% ============================================================

\begin{jargon}{Side effect}
An action with a lasting, observable consequence outside the running program: writing
a file, updating a spreadsheet, adding a calendar entry, drafting an email. Contrast a
\emph{read-only} action (reading a file, listing a directory), which can be safely
retried because it changes nothing. Every tool in this project declares a boolean
\code{side\_effect} flag; only side-effecting tools are gated.
\end{jargon}

\code{RunState.unfinished\_calls()} returns every requested tool call still
\code{requested} or \code{approved}, in request order. \code{\_process\_tool\_calls()}
walks them:

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
% Laid out vertically on purpose: the horizontal version had to be shrunk so far to
% fit the text width that the labels stopped being readable.
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=0.95cm and 0.7cm]
  \node[box, minimum width=4cm] (call) {next unfinished tool call};
  \node[dec, below=of call] (se) {\code{side\_effect}\\true?};
  \node[dec, below=1.15cm of se] (dec2) {already\\approved?};
  \node[dec, below=1.15cm of dec2] (pol) {approval policy\\returns?};

  \node[box, minimum width=3.2cm, right=2.6cm of se, draw=okgreen] (exec1)
    {execute now};

  \node[box, below=1.3cm of pol, draw=okgreen, minimum width=3.4cm] (app)
    {append \code{tool\_approved},\\then execute};
  \node[box, left=of app, draw=warnred, minimum width=3.4cm] (wait)
    {pause the run:\\\code{waiting\_approval}};
  \node[box, right=of app, draw=warnred, minimum width=3.4cm] (den)
    {append \code{tool\_denied}\\no execution;\\the model is told};

  \draw[flow] (call) -- (se);
  \draw[flow] (se) -- node[lbl, pos=0.45]{no, read-only} (exec1);
  \draw[flow] (se) -- node[lbl]{yes} (dec2);
  \draw[flow] (dec2) -| node[lbl, pos=0.25]{yes} (exec1);
  \draw[flow] (dec2) -- node[lbl]{no} (pol);
  \draw[flow] (pol.south) -- node[lbl, pos=0.55]{approve} (app.north);
  \draw[flow] (pol.west) -| node[lbl, pos=0.25]{\code{None}} (wait.north);
  \draw[flow] (pol.east) -| node[lbl, pos=0.25]{deny} (den.north);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The gate. A read-only call never reaches the policy. \code{None} is the
interesting return: it means ``a human must decide'', and the loop returns immediately
without touching any later call.}
\end{figure}

An \emph{approval policy} is just a function from a tool call to one of three answers:
\code{("approve", who)}, \code{("deny", who, reason)}, or \code{None} meaning ask a
human. That is the whole abstraction. The CLI's \code{--auto-approve} passes a function
returning approve; the default passes no policy at all, which pauses; a scenario can
pass one that denies specific tools by name.

\begin{keyidea}{The gate is safe because of ordering, not because of a lock}
The pause is not a thread blocking on a condition variable, and there is no in-memory
``pending approvals'' queue. The run simply stops and the process can exit. The pending
approval exists only as a fact about the log: a \code{tool\_requested} event with
\code{side\_effect: true} and no matching decision event after it. Any process, minutes
or days later, can read the log and see it. That is why the CLI and the web UI can both
serve the same approval queue without sharing anything but the database file.
\end{keyidea}

\code{approve\_call()} and \code{deny\_call()} in \file{runtime.py} are the out-of-band
entry points the CLI and web UI use. Both call \code{\_assert\_pending()} first, which
re-projects the log and raises \code{ValueError} unless the call really is pending, so
approving the same call twice, or approving a call that was already denied, is rejected
rather than silently appending a contradictory event.

% ============================================================
\section{Idempotency and the crash window}
% ============================================================

\begin{jargon}{Idempotency}
An operation is \emph{idempotent} if doing it twice has the same effect as doing it
once. Re-reading a file is naturally idempotent. Sending a reminder email is not:
sending it twice annoys the customer twice. This project makes tool execution
idempotent \emph{per run} by remembering that it already ran.
\end{jargon}

\code{ToolExecutor.execute()} derives a key from \code{(run\_id, seq of the
tool\_requested event)}:

\begin{lstlisting}[language=Python]
def idempotency_key(run_id, requested_seq):
    return hashlib.sha256(f"{run_id}:{requested_seq}".encode()).hexdigest()[:32]
\end{lstlisting}

Both inputs are identical whether this is the first attempt or a resume after a crash,
so the key is stable. That is the whole trick, and it is why the key is derived from
the log rather than generated: a generated id would be lost in the crash it is meant to
survive.

The write order is deliberate:

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=0.55cm and 0.4cm]
  \node[box, minimum width=7cm] (h) {1. run the tool handler\\\emph{the side effect happens here}};
  \node[box, minimum width=7cm, below=of h] (l)
    {2. \code{claim\_execution()}: \code{INSERT OR IGNORE}\\into the \code{executions} ledger};
  \node[box, minimum width=7cm, below=of l] (a)
    {3. the runtime appends \code{tool\_executed}};
  \draw[flow] (h) -- (l);
  \draw[flow] (l) -- (a);
  \draw[decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=6pt}, thick, draw=okgreen]
    ($(a.north east)+(0.25,0.1)$) -- ($(l.south east)+(0.25,-0.1)$)
    node[midway, right=8pt, align=left, font=\small, text=okgreen]
    {\textbf{closed} window:\\resume finds the ledger\\entry and does not re-run};
  \draw[decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=6pt}, thick, draw=warnred]
    ($(l.north west)+(-0.25,0.1)$) -- ($(h.south west)+(-0.25,-0.1)$)
    node[midway, left=8pt, align=right, font=\small, text=warnred]
    {\textbf{open} window:\\side effect done,\\nothing recorded};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The execution order in \file{tools/executor.py}, and the two windows it
creates. Only the lower one is closed.}
\end{figure}

If the process dies between step 2 and step 3, a subsequent resume calls
\code{execute()} again with the same key, finds the row already in the ledger via
\code{get\_execution()}, and returns that recorded result without touching the handler.
The runtime then appends \code{tool\_executed} with \code{replayed: true}, so the log
records that this execution did not actually re-run. \file{tests/test\_resume.py}
exercises exactly this and asserts a side-effect counter stays at 1.

\begin{honest}{The window between the handler and the ledger is open, and untested}
The README says: ``A crash between `handler ran' and `tool\_executed appended' is where
resume would naively re-execute. The executor writes the result to an \code{INSERT OR
IGNORE} ledger \dots before the runtime appends the event.'' That is true, and it
closes most of the window. It does not close all of it.

Read \code{ToolExecutor.execute()} closely. The handler returns, and the ledger write
happens on the very next line:

\begin{lstlisting}[language=Python]
result = self._run_with_timeout(spec, args)   # the side effect happens here
self.store.claim_execution(key, run_id, result)   # ... and is recorded here
\end{lstlisting}

A crash between those two statements leaves the side effect done and nothing at all
recorded, and a resumed run will run the handler a second time. The email goes out
twice.

The test does not cover this. \code{CrashAfterExecute} in \file{tests/test\_resume.py}
wraps the executor and raises \emph{after calling \code{self.inner.execute(...)} in
full}, which includes the ledger write. So the test crashes in the closed window, which
is why the assertion passes. Its docstring calls it ``the worst possible crash point
for duplicate side effects'', and that is the one thing it is not: the genuinely worst
point is one line earlier, inside \code{execute()}, and nothing tests it.

This is defensible, and worth being able to defend precisely rather than deny. The gap
is inherent: you cannot atomically perform a real-world side effect and a local
database write, and no amount of reordering fixes that. Shrinking the window is the
best a local ledger can do. Genuinely closing it requires the side effect itself to
carry the idempotency key so that the \emph{remote} system deduplicates, which is what
payment APIs do with an \code{Idempotency-Key} header. The honest claim is ``the
duplicate window is reduced from the whole tool execution to two adjacent statements'',
not ``no duplicate side effects''.
\end{honest}

The executor also distinguishes two kinds of failure, which buys the retry policy for
free:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \code{ToolError} is an \emph{expected}, deterministic refusal: a path outside
  the sandbox, a host not on the allowlist, a missing file, a cell out of range.
  Retrying cannot help, so it fails immediately.
  \item \code{ToolTimeout} (a subclass of \code{ToolError}, caught first) and any other
  exception are treated as transient and consume the retry budget with backoff.
\end{itemize}

\begin{honest}{Tool timeouts do not stop the tool (the README states this one)}
\code{\_run\_with\_timeout} submits the handler to a one-worker
\code{ThreadPoolExecutor} and calls \code{future.result(timeout=...)}. When that times
out the code calls \code{future.cancel()}, which for an \emph{already running} task
returns \code{False} and does nothing: Python cannot kill a running thread. The handler
keeps running to completion in the background, invisible.

Three consequences the README names two of. A genuinely hung tool leaks a thread per
attempt. A timed-out side-effecting tool may still complete its side effect after the
runtime has recorded it as failed. And, not in the README: the
\code{with ... ThreadPoolExecutor} block cannot exit until the abandoned worker
finishes, because \code{\_\_exit\_\_} calls \code{shutdown(wait=True)}, so a hung tool
hangs \code{execute()} itself rather than returning after \code{spec.timeout}. The
timeout is honest about ``how long do I wait for an answer'' only when the tool
eventually returns. Subprocess isolation is the real fix, and the README says so.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{Deterministic replay}
% ============================================================

\subsection{What deterministic replay means, and why an agent needs it}

\begin{jargon}{Deterministic}
A process is \emph{deterministic} if the same inputs always produce the same outputs.
Adding two numbers is deterministic. Reading the clock is not. Asking an LLM a question
is emphatically not.
\end{jargon}

\begin{jargon}{Deterministic replay}
Re-executing a program's logic using only what was recorded about a previous execution,
with no live external calls, and checking that the result matches what was recorded.
Every input the program would normally fetch from the outside world (the next model
turn, a tool's result, a human's decision, the wall clock) is served instead from the
recording.
\end{jargon}

Here is why this matters more for an agent than for ordinary software. Suppose run 4187
drafted an email to the wrong customer, and you want to know why.

You cannot just run it again. The model is nondeterministic, so the second run may
behave perfectly and tell you nothing. It costs money each time you try. And running it
again would draft \emph{another} email, so your debugging tool has side effects. The
usual approach of ``add a print statement and re-run'' is unavailable on all three
counts.

Replay dissolves the problem. The log already contains every nondeterministic input the
run received. Feed those recorded inputs back into the same loop and the run is now a
pure function: it must produce the same events, every time, for free, with no email
sent. You can step through it, and you can do it a year later.

\begin{keyidea}{Determinism is a budget you spend field by field}
The reason replay works here is not a clever replay engine. It is that every event
payload was designed so replay can recompute it. Any value that is not a pure function
of the log is a future divergence: a wall clock reading, a random id, an attempt count
that depends on real sleeps, a ``request sent at'' timestamp.

So the store takes an injectable clock, sleeps are injectable and become no-ops under
replay, and \code{model\_requested} records a \emph{digest} of the messages rather than
anything time-dependent. Each of those is a small design concession made specifically to
keep replay exact. The comparator can then demand byte equality instead of some fuzzy
``close enough'' match, which is the difference between a real regression tripwire and a
test that nobody trusts.
\end{keyidea}

\subsection{The four stand-ins}

\code{replay\_run()} builds four objects, each substituting for a real dependency, all
reading from the same source event list:

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=0.75cm and 1.5cm]
  \node[store, minimum width=2.6cm] (src) {the original\\event log\\(read only)};

  \node[extbox, right=2.1cm of src, yshift=2.4cm] (clock)
    {\code{\_RecordedClock}\\replaces \code{time.time}};
  \node[extbox, right=2.1cm of src, yshift=0.8cm] (adapt)
    {\code{\_RecordedAdapter}\\replaces the live model};
  \node[extbox, right=2.1cm of src, yshift=-0.8cm] (exe)
    {\code{\_RecordedExecutor}\\replaces tool handlers};
  \node[extbox, right=2.1cm of src, yshift=-2.4cm] (appr)
    {\code{\_RecordedApprovals}\\replaces the human};

  \node[box, fill=softgrey, draw=accent, very thick, right=2.1cm of exe, yshift=0.8cm,
        align=center] (rt) {the \textbf{real},\\unmodified\\\code{Runtime.drive()}};

  \node[store, below=1.5cm of rt, minimum width=2.8cm] (out)
    {a new run:\\\code{<id>-replay-<rnd>}};

  \draw[dflow] (src.east) to[out=0,in=180] (clock.west);
  \draw[dflow] (src.east) to[out=0,in=180] (adapt.west);
  \draw[dflow] (src.east) to[out=0,in=180] (exe.west);
  \draw[dflow] (src.east) to[out=0,in=180] (appr.west);

  \draw[flow] (clock.east) to[out=0,in=180] (rt.west);
  \draw[flow] (adapt.east) to[out=0,in=180] (rt.west);
  \draw[flow] (exe.east) to[out=0,in=180] (rt.west);
  \draw[flow] (appr.east) to[out=0,in=180] (rt.west);

  \draw[flow] (rt) -- node[lbl]{appends} (out);

  % The comparator sits below, fed by both logs.
  \node[dec, below=1.5cm of src, aspect=2.2, xshift=1.2cm] (cmp)
    {canonical logs\\equal?};
  \node[box, draw=okgreen, below=1.0cm of cmp, xshift=-1.9cm, minimum width=3cm]
    (yes) {byte-identical: yes};
  \node[box, draw=warnred, below=1.0cm of cmp, xshift=2.1cm, minimum width=3cm]
    (no) {first divergence\\at seq N};

  \draw[dflow] (src.south) -- node[lbl, pos=0.6]{original} (cmp.north);
  \draw[dflow] (out.west) -- node[lbl, pos=0.5]{replayed} (cmp.east);
  \draw[flow] (cmp.south) -- node[lbl, pos=0.7]{yes} (yes.north);
  \draw[flow] (cmp.south) -- node[lbl, pos=0.7]{no} (no.north);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The replay path. Note what is \emph{not} swapped: the runtime itself. That is
the point. If the loop were reimplemented for replay, replay would prove nothing about
the loop.}
\end{figure}

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{3.4cm}p{4.3cm}p{5.6cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Stand-in} & \textbf{Replaces} & \textbf{Replays from} \\
\midrule
\code{\_RecordedClock} & \code{time.time} & the exact \code{ts} of each original event, handed out in append order \\
\code{\_RecordedAdapter} & the live \code{ModelAdapter} & each \code{model\_error} or \code{model\_responded} event in order, re-raising errors and malformed outputs exactly as before \\
\code{\_RecordedExecutor} & \code{ToolExecutor} & the \code{tool\_executed} or \code{tool\_failed} outcome recorded for each \code{tool\_requested} sequence number \\
\code{\_RecordedApprovals} & the approval policy & the \code{tool\_approved} or \code{tool\_denied} decision recorded per call id \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The clock deserves a second look, because it is the subtlest of the four. It is not a
frozen time or a fake counter: it is a \emph{queue} of the original timestamps, handed
out one per \code{append()} call. This only works because appends happen in exactly the
same order on replay. If they did not, timestamps would attach to the wrong events and
the comparison would fail. So the recorded clock is not merely a convenience for making
the diff pass; it is itself an assertion that the append order was reproduced.

\subsection{Verified: it actually replays byte-identically}

Running the real thing, not quoting the README:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ agentrun replay 46bd97a45451
replayed 15/15 events into 46bd97a45451-replay-8765c6
byte-identical: yes

$ agentrun replay 46bd97a45451 --until 6
replayed 7/7 events into 46bd97a45451-replay-7c1c16
byte-identical: yes
state at event 6: status=running, model_calls=2, tools_executed=['read_file']
\end{lstlisting}

\code{--until <seq>} truncates the source log before replaying, giving a snapshot of the
run's state at that exact point. Truncating can leave the loop asking for a model turn
that was never recorded; rather than special-casing the loop, the stand-ins raise when
exhausted and \code{replay\_run()} treats that as the stop condition.

\begin{honest}{The truncation catch swallows every \code{RuntimeError}, not just exhaustion}
\code{replay\_run()} wraps \code{rt.start(...)} in \code{try: ... except RuntimeError:
pass}. The intent, per the comment, is that a truncated log leaves the stand-ins
exhausted and that is a normal stop.

But the catch is unconditional (it is not gated on \code{until is not None}) and
\code{RuntimeError} is exactly what three unrelated failures raise: the clock's
``replay produced more events than the original run'', the adapter's ``replay adapter
exhausted'', and \code{EventStore.append}'s ``could not append event for run''. A
genuine bug in any of those is silently swallowed and reported as a normal stop.

The blast radius is limited, because the comparator runs afterwards and a truncated
replay of a full log would come back as ``not identical''. So the failure surfaces, just
mislabeled: you would be told the logs diverge at seq N when what actually happened is
that the store could not write. Catching the specific exhaustion condition, or gating
the catch on \code{until}, would cost one line.
\end{honest}

\subsection{Replay writes into the same database, and that has consequences}

The replayed run is not a scratch object. \code{replay\_run()} opens a second
\code{EventStore} on \emph{the same database file} and appends a complete new run under
the id \code{<original>-replay-<random>}. Nothing is cleaned up afterwards.

This is visible in the repository itself. The tracked file \file{agentrun.db} contains
45 events across 3 run ids: one real run and two replays of it, matching the transcript
in \file{docs/screenshots/run-output.txt} exactly. Every replay you ever run
permanently doubles a run's storage and adds a row to \code{agentrun runs list}, which
was verified:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ agentrun runs list
46bd97a45451                finished   ops_assistant   15 events  $0.0032 simulated ...
46bd97a45451-replay-7c1c16  running    ops_assistant    7 events  $0.0005 simulated ...
46bd97a45451-replay-8765c6  finished   ops_assistant   15 events  $0.0032 simulated ...
\end{lstlisting}

That is untidy. The next finding is not.

\begin{honest}{A truncated replay can create a phantom approval that executes a real side effect. Verified.}
This is the most serious thing in this document, and it was reproduced end to end.

A replay run is a real run in every way the rest of the system can detect. Its
\code{run\_created} event copies the original's \code{meta} verbatim
(\code{meta=created.get("meta", \{\})}), including the real \code{workspace} path and
\code{scenario\_path}. The only thing marking it as a replay is a substring in its run
id. Nothing in \file{projection.py}, \file{cli.py}, \file{web/app.py} or
\file{bootstrap.py} looks for that substring.

Now replay with \code{--until} truncated exactly at a pending side-effecting call. The
replayed run stops, correctly, at \code{waiting\_approval}. It is now a non-terminal run
with a pending approval sitting in the shared database:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ agentrun replay d5ca78675af3 --until 8
replayed 9/9 events into d5ca78675af3-replay-6c2258
byte-identical: yes
state at event 8: status=waiting_approval, model_calls=2, tools_executed=['read_file']

$ agentrun approvals list
d5ca78675af3-replay-6c2258  call-2-0  draft_email  {'to': 'billing@acme.example', ...}
\end{lstlisting}

The operator's approval queue now lists a call that no live run is waiting on,
indistinguishable from a real one. Approve it and \code{approvals approve} calls
\code{resume\_run()}, which rebuilds a runtime from that copied meta: a \emph{real}
executor, over the \emph{real} workspace. The gate opens and the tool fires for real.
Confirmed by deleting the outbox first, so that a reappearing file could only mean the
handler genuinely ran:

\begin{lstlisting}
$ rm -rf ws/outbox
$ agentrun approvals approve d5ca78675af3-replay-6c2258 call-2-0
run d5ca78675af3-replay-6c2258: finished
  final answer: Drafted a reminder email to Acme Corp for overdue invoice INV-1001.
$ ls ws/outbox/
Overdue_invoice_INV-1001.eml      <- written again, by a "replay"
\end{lstlisting}

The idempotency ledger cannot help: its key is \code{(run\_id, seq)} and the replay's
run id is different by construction, so the ledger sees a brand new call.

This directly undercuts the project's headline claim. ``Replay: no network, no tool
handlers, no key needed'' is true \emph{during} replay. It is not true afterwards,
because a truncated replay leaves behind a live, resumable run that is no longer a
replay and that no longer knows it ever was one.

Two changes would each independently close it, and both are small: put a
\code{replay\_of: <run\_id>} field in the replayed \code{run\_created} payload and have
\code{resume\_run}, \code{approvals list} and the web UI refuse or hide such runs; or
replay into a throwaway database rather than the live one, which also fixes the
storage growth and the polluted run list. The second is cleaner: a replay is a
question, not a fact, and it has no business being written next to real runs at all.

Scope, stated fairly: a \emph{full} replay ends in a terminal state and leaves no
pending approval, so only \code{--until} triggers this, and \code{--until} is a
debugging flag. The operator must also approve a call whose run id visibly contains
\code{-replay-}. But the entire premise of an approval queue is that operators approve
what it lists.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{Failure injection}
% ============================================================

\begin{jargon}{Fault injection}
Deliberately making a system behave as if something went wrong (a timeout, a malformed
response, a server error) in a controlled test, to verify the system handles that
failure the way it is supposed to, instead of only ever testing the happy path.
\end{jargon}

\file{faults.py} defines \code{FaultPlan}, a plain dataclass loadable straight from a
scenario's YAML \code{faults:} block:

\begin{lstlisting}
faults:
  model:
    - {at_call: 2, kind: malformed}      # or kind: adapter_500
  tools:
    read_file:
      - {at_attempt: 1, kind: exception, message: "disk error"}
      - {at_attempt: 2, kind: timeout}
\end{lstlisting}

\code{model\_fault(call\_number)} is consulted by \code{MockModelAdapter.complete()} on
every invocation; \code{at\_call} counts \emph{every} adapter call, including ones that
themselves fail. \code{tool\_fault(tool\_name, attempt)} is the executor's
\code{fault\_hook}, consulted before each attempt of a given tool. Both let a scenario
exercise ``fails once, then a clean retry succeeds'' without a real failing service.
Unknown fault kinds are rejected at load time by \code{from\_dict}.

The runtime's contract under faults: adapter errors retry with backoff then fail the run
with a recorded cause; malformed output earns a corrective observation twice, then fails
the run; tool failures surface to the model as ordinary error observations. Nothing
raises out of \code{drive()}. \file{tests/test\_faults.py} pins this down.

\subsection{The two-counter bug, and why it is the interesting one}

The README singles out a bug worth understanding, because the fix is what makes fault
plans work at all. \code{MockModelAdapter} keeps two counters:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \code{calls}: every invocation of \code{complete()}, including ones a fault plan
  turns into an injected error. This is what \code{at\_call} matches against.
  \item \code{script\_i}: how many script turns were actually \emph{consumed}.
\end{itemize}

The first design used one counter for both. An injected 500 on call 1 silently consumed
script turn 1, so every later turn was off by one and every scenario with a fault tested
the wrong thing. Splitting them fixed it: injected faults advance \code{calls} but not
\code{script\_i}, so the script continues where it left off once the fault clears.

Verified directly, by driving the adapter with a fault plan and reading both counters:
an injected malformed fault leaves \code{calls=1, script\_i=0}. The script was not
touched. Section 18 shows the other half of this story, which is not fixed.

% ============================================================
\section{Cost and latency accounting}
% ============================================================

Every \code{model\_responded} carries a \code{usage} object: input and output token
counts, latency in milliseconds, and a \code{simulated} boolean.
\code{accounting.py}'s \code{account(events)} folds these into per-run totals using a
hardcoded price table in USD per million tokens:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}lrr@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Model} & \textbf{Input \$/1M tok} & \textbf{Output \$/1M tok} \\
\midrule
\code{mock-1} & 3.00 & 15.00 \\
\code{claude-sonnet-4-5} & 3.00 & 15.00 \\
\code{gpt-4o} & 2.50 & 10.00 \\
(anything else) & 3.00 & 15.00 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The mock adapter fabricates deterministic but fake usage from message sizes, and
\code{RunCosts.simulated} is true whenever any usage in the run came from it. The CLI
and web UI both print ``(simulated)'' next to such a cost so it is never mistaken for a
real bill. This was verified in the live output above: \code{cost: \$0.0021
(simulated)}.

\begin{honest}{Two small accounting inaccuracies}
Neither is serious; both are the kind of thing worth knowing before someone asks.

\code{costs.model = model or costs.model} keeps the \emph{last} model seen. A run whose
adapter changed mid-flight (possible: \code{bootstrap} rebuilds the adapter from meta on
every resume) reports one model name for the whole run. The per-event cost arithmetic is
still correct, since each event is priced with its own model; only the reported label is
lossy.

The default price silently applies to unknown models. A run against a model not in the
table produces a confident dollar figure computed from Claude Sonnet's prices, with
nothing in the output saying so. Given the README already concedes the table ``will
drift'', a \code{price\_source: default} marker in \code{RunCosts} would cost little.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{Tools: registry, executor, built-ins}
% ============================================================

\begin{jargon}{JSON Schema}
A specification language for describing the shape of JSON data: which fields exist,
their types, which are required. Tool arguments are declared this way so badly formed
arguments are rejected before a handler runs, and so the same declaration can be handed
to an LLM API to tell it exactly what the tool expects. One declaration, two consumers.
\end{jargon}

\code{ToolSpec} captures, per tool: \code{name}, \code{description}, a JSON-schema
\code{parameters} definition, the Python \code{handler}, the \code{side\_effect} flag, a
\code{timeout}, a \code{retries} count and a \code{backoff} base. \code{ToolRegistry}
validates each schema at registration time with
\code{jsonschema.Draft202012Validator.check\_schema}, rejects duplicate names, and
exposes \code{subset(names)}, used to restrict a run to only the tools its agent is
allowed (Section 14).

\begin{jargon}{Sandbox}
A restricted execution environment that limits what a program can access, to contain
the effect of something going wrong or being misused. Here every filesystem tool
resolves paths through \code{ToolContext.resolve()}, which calls \code{Path.resolve()}
(collapsing any \code{..} segments and following symlinks) and then rejects the path
unless it is the workspace root itself or has the workspace root among its parents. So
a model cannot be talked into reading \code{../../etc/passwd}.
\end{jargon}

\subsection{The eight built-in tools}

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{2.4cm}p{6.3cm}p{1.4cm}p{2.9cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Tool} & \textbf{What it does} & \textbf{Side effect?} & \textbf{Notes} \\
\midrule
\code{read\_file} & read a UTF-8 text file & no & content truncated to 20{,}000 chars \\
\code{write\_file} & write a UTF-8 text file & \textbf{yes} & creates parent dirs \\
\code{list\_files} & recursively list files under a directory & no & capped at 500 names \\
\code{http\_get} & GET a URL & no & host must be on an explicit allowlist, else \code{ToolError}; 15s timeout \\
\code{csv\_update} & \code{append\_row} or \code{set\_cell} on a CSV & \textbf{yes} & out-of-range cell is a \code{ToolError} \\
\code{draft\_email} & write an RFC 5322 \code{.eml} into the workspace \file{outbox/} & \textbf{yes} & never sends; filename sanitized from the subject \\
\code{calendar\_add} & insert a row into a per-workspace SQLite \file{calendar.db} & \textbf{yes} & \\
\code{calendar\_list} & read the calendar back, ordered by start time & no & \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\begin{jargon}{Allowlist}
A list of explicitly permitted values (here, hostnames) where everything not on the list
is rejected by default. The opposite of a blocklist, and the safer default for anything
reachable by a semi-autonomous actor: a blocklist fails open on the case you did not
think of, an allowlist fails closed.
\end{jargon}

\begin{honest}{\code{http\_get} is marked read-only, which is a judgment call, not a fact}
\code{http\_get} has \code{side\_effect=False}, so it never reaches the approval gate.
An HTTP GET is \emph{conventionally} safe, but nothing enforces that: a GET can trigger
anything the server chooses, and \code{follow\_redirects=True} means the allowlist is
checked against the URL the model asked for, not against wherever it ends up. An
allowlisted host that redirects offsite is fetched without a second check.

In practice the allowlist defaults to empty (\code{AGENTRUN\_HTTP\_ALLOWLIST}
unset blocks every host), so the exposure needs an operator to opt in. Worth naming
anyway: this is the one tool whose safety rests on a convention about the wider world
rather than on a check in this codebase.
\end{honest}

\begin{honest}{\code{draft\_email} silently overwrites drafts with the same subject}
The \code{.eml} filename is derived only from the subject, sanitized and truncated to 60
characters. Two drafts to \emph{different recipients} with the same subject, or two
subjects agreeing in their first 60 characters, resolve to the same path and the second
overwrites the first with no error. The tool's returned \code{draft\_path} then names a
file whose contents belong to a different email than the one the log says was drafted.
The audit trail and the artifact disagree, which is a poor property for the one tool the
approval gate exists to guard.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{Model adapters}
% ============================================================

\begin{jargon}{Adapter}
A class implementing one interface (\code{ModelAdapter.complete(messages, tools)
$\rightarrow$ ModelTurn}) around a specific way of getting the next turn: a real API, or
a scripted fake. The rest of the runtime only ever talks to this interface, never to a
provider's request or response shape directly. That is what makes the mock adapter
possible, and the mock adapter is what makes the whole project testable offline.
\end{jargon}

\file{adapters/base.py} defines the shared \code{ModelTurn} (text, tool calls,
\code{Usage}, model name, with \code{is\_final} true exactly when there are no tool
calls) and the two exceptions the runtime catches by name: \code{AdapterError}
(transport or provider failure, retryable) and \code{MalformedOutputError} (output that
could not be parsed at all, carrying the raw text for diagnosis).

\subsection{\code{MockModelAdapter}}

\file{adapters/mock.py} plays back a \emph{script}: an ordered list of turns from a
scenario YAML, each either \code{tool\_calls}, \code{final} text, or \code{malformed}
raw garbage. Usage numbers are fabricated deterministically from message and output size
and always carry \code{simulated: true}. Call ids are generated as
\code{call-\{calls\}-\{i\}}, which is where the \code{call-2-0} in the transcripts comes
from: the second adapter call, first tool call in it.

\begin{honest}{``Surfaced as a hard error'' is not what happens when a script runs out}
The comment above the script-exhausted branch says running past the end of a script ``is
a scenario bug, surfaced as a hard error rather than a guess''. But the code raises
\code{MalformedOutputError}, and \file{runtime.py} catches that by name and handles it
gracefully: it records the run as having received malformed model output, feeds the
model a corrective ``your previous output could not be parsed'' message, and after
\code{max\_malformed} tries fails the run with cause \code{malformed\_model\_output:
mock script exhausted after N turns}.

So a scenario authoring mistake is reported as a \emph{model behavior} problem. The
cause string does carry the real reason, which saves it, but the run's shape (two
corrective retries, then a malformed failure) is the shape of a misbehaving model, not
of a too-short script. A distinct exception type that \code{drive()} does not catch
would be a hard error; \code{MalformedOutputError} is by construction the one thing that
cannot be.
\end{honest}

\subsection{\code{AnthropicAdapter} and \code{OpenAIAdapter}}

Both implement request building and response parsing for their provider:
\code{POST /v1/messages} (Anthropic Messages API) and
\code{POST /v1/chat/completions} (OpenAI Chat Completions), keyed by
\code{ANTHROPIC\_API\_KEY} and \code{OPENAI\_API\_KEY}, over \code{httpx}. Each
translates the neutral message list into its provider's wire shape: Anthropic's
\code{tool\_use} and \code{tool\_result} content blocks (where a tool result is carried
on a \code{user} message) versus OpenAI's \code{tool\_calls} array and a dedicated
\code{tool} role. Both map HTTP $\geq$ 500 and other non-200 responses to
\code{AdapterError}, and an unexpected response shape to \code{MalformedOutputError}.

Both take an injectable \code{client}, which is what makes them testable with no
network: \file{tests/test\_adapters.py} passes a fake \code{httpx.Client}.

\begin{jargon}{Contract test}
A test that checks a piece of code produces the exact request shape a real API expects
and correctly parses the exact response shape that API returns, without calling the real
API. It verifies this side of the contract between two systems. It cannot verify the
other side: if the provider's real API differs from what the test assumes, the test still
passes.
\end{jargon}

\begin{honest}{The live adapters are unverified, and the README says so}
Quoting the project's own scope note: ``The Anthropic and OpenAI adapters are implemented
against their current APIs and covered by offline contract tests (request building,
response parsing, error mapping via injected HTTP transports), but \textbf{no live API
calls were made here because no API keys were available}. Everything else, including the
full test suite, the CLI and the web UI, runs and was verified entirely offline.''

This document confirms the second half by running it (Section 21) and cannot confirm the
first half either. The honest reading: the contract tests prove the adapters are
self-consistent, not that they are correct against the real APIs. What would actually be
caught by a live call and not by these tests: a required header, a renamed field, a
changed enum value, an auth detail, a tool-schema constraint the provider enforces.
That is a real gap, and the README is right to name it rather than imply coverage the
tests do not give.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{Agents and scenarios}
% ============================================================

\file{agents.py} defines three agents, each a fixed system prompt plus a restricted tool
subset. The subset is enforced by \code{registry.subset()}, so a tool a agent is not
granted is not merely discouraged by the prompt, it is not in the registry the adapter
is shown, and a call to it would append \code{tool\_failed} with ``unknown tool''.

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{3.2cm}p{10.4cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Agent} & \textbf{Role and allowed tools} \\
\midrule
\code{ops\_assistant} & Chases overdue invoices, drafts (never sends) reminder emails, keeps an invoice CSV current. Tools: \code{read\_file}, \code{list\_files}, \code{csv\_update}, \code{draft\_email}, \code{calendar\_add}, \code{calendar\_list}. \\[0.3em]
\code{data\_entry} & Transcribes records into CSV sheets, verifying by reading back. Tools: \code{read\_file}, \code{list\_files}, \code{write\_file}, \code{csv\_update}. \\[0.3em]
\code{research\_summarizer} & Fetches allowlisted pages and local notes, writes a sourced summary. Tools: \code{read\_file}, \code{list\_files}, \code{http\_get}, \code{write\_file}. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The repository ships 24 scenario YAML files, 8 per agent folder, counted directly from
the tree and confirmed by the suite reporting 24 scenarios when run. The README's
``Challenges'' section notes this count was itself once wrong and was corrected by
checking the files; it is right now.

% ============================================================
\section{Scenario regression testing}
% ============================================================

\begin{jargon}{Regression test}
A test that exists specifically to catch something that used to work and now behaves
differently. Here, the thing that changes is not the code: it is the model, or the
prompt. That is the point.
\end{jargon}

\begin{keyidea}{What is actually being tested}
This is the part most agent demos have no answer for. You cannot unit-test ``the agent
does the right thing'', because the agent's behavior lives in a model you do not
control and cannot pin. So this project tests the loop against a \emph{recorded}
behavior instead: a scenario names one or more behavior versions, each a full script of
what the model would say, and asserts what the runtime does with it.

That reframes the question from ``is the agent correct'' (unanswerable) to ``if the
model behaves like \emph{this}, does the runtime do the right thing, and does behavior
\code{v2} differ from \code{v1}, and where exactly'' (answerable, offline, in seconds,
for free).
\end{keyidea}

Each scenario YAML is validated against a strict JSON schema in \file{loader.py} with
\code{additionalProperties: False} throughout, so a typo in a key is a load error rather
than a silently ignored assertion. \code{load\_pack} also rejects duplicate scenario
names across the tree. A second, hand-written check enforces what JSON Schema expresses
awkwardly: each behavior turn must have exactly one of \code{tool\_calls}, \code{final}
or \code{malformed}.

\subsection{Assertions}

\code{check\_expectations()} evaluates every assertion present and never short-circuits,
so a failing scenario reports everything wrong at once:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{5.5cm}p{7.9cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Assertion key} & \textbf{Checks} \\
\midrule
\code{status} & final run status \\
\code{tools\_executed} & the exact, ordered list of tools that executed successfully \\
\code{tools\_executed\_contains} & these tools ran somewhere (order and extras ignored) \\
\code{tools\_not\_executed} & these tools never ran \\
\code{denied\_tools} & these tools were denied at the gate \\
\code{final\_contains} / \code{final\_not\_contains} & case-insensitive substring checks on the final answer \\
\code{failure\_cause\_contains} & substring of the recorded failure cause \\
\code{files\_exist} & workspace-relative paths that must exist afterwards \\
\code{min\_events} & minimum total event count \\
\code{no\_side\_effect\_before\_approval} & every executed side-effecting call has a \code{tool\_approved} event at a \emph{lower seq} than its \code{tool\_executed} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The last one is the safety property of the whole project, expressed as an assertion over
the log. Its own comment concedes it ``always holds by construction; the assertion
documents it per scenario''. That is a fair description and a reasonable thing to
assert anyway: it is a tripwire on the construction, and it costs nothing.

\subsection{Verified: running the suite}

\begin{lstlisting}
$ agentrun test scenarios/ --behavior v1
behavior v1: 24/24 scenarios passed
  [PASS] batch-entry-with-listing (20 events, status=finished, cost=$0.0032 simulated)
  [PASS] create-new-sheet (10 events, status=finished, cost=$0.0012 simulated)
  ...

$ agentrun test scenarios/ --behavior v1 --compare v2
comparing v1 -> v2: v2 fails 3 of 24 scenarios (3 regressions vs v1)
  [REGRESSION] chase-overdue-acme
      first divergence at event 3: expected tool_requested:read_file,
                                        got tool_requested:draft_email
      v2 failure: tools_executed: expected ['read_file', 'draft_email'],
                                       got ['draft_email']
  [REGRESSION] transcribe-two-records
      first divergence at event 14: expected tool_requested:csv_update, got run_finished
  [REGRESSION] summarize-notes
      first divergence at event 8: expected tool_requested:write_file, got run_finished
\end{lstlisting}

The \code{v2} behaviors are deliberately degraded: \code{chase-overdue-acme}'s
\code{v2} drafts the email \emph{without reading the invoice first}, which is exactly
the kind of regression a new model version might introduce and exactly the kind a
pass/fail assertion alone would describe uselessly. The diff names the event.

The mechanism is deliberately unsophisticated and it is worth defending as such.
\code{event\_signature()} reduces each event to a \code{"type:tool-or-call-id"} string;
\code{diff\_results()} walks two signature lists in lock-step and reports the first
index where they differ. No diffing library, no heuristics. For an ordered list of short
strings, first-mismatch is the correct and complete answer. The README's claim that ``a
per-event type:detail signature is enough for useful regression diffs'' is borne out by
the output above, which is genuinely the most useful line in the whole tool.

% ============================================================
\section{The command line interface}
% ============================================================

\file{cli.py} exposes a single \code{agentrun} entry point registered via
\file{pyproject.toml}'s \code{[project.scripts]}, built with \textbf{Click}, a library
for building command line tools out of decorated functions.

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{5.2cm}p{8.2cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Command} & \textbf{What it does} \\
\midrule
\code{agentrun run --scenario ...} & start a mock-adapter run scripted by a scenario; \code{--auto-approve} skips the gate \\
\code{agentrun resume <run-id>} & rebuild the runtime from \code{run\_created} meta and drive forward \\
\code{agentrun replay <run-id> [--until N]} & deterministic replay; exits nonzero if not byte-identical \\
\code{agentrun runs list} / \code{show} & list runs with status and cost, or print one run's canonical log \\
\code{agentrun approvals list|approve|deny} & manage the approval queue; \code{deny --reason} \\
\code{agentrun test <pack> [--behavior --compare --html]} & the scenario suite \\
\code{agentrun serve [--host --port]} & the FastAPI web UI via \code{uvicorn} \\
\code{agentrun agents} & list agent definitions and their tools \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Every command takes \code{--db} (default \code{agentrun.db}, overridable by
\code{AGENTRUN\_DB}). Both \code{replay} and \code{test} exit nonzero on failure, which
is what makes them usable in a CI pipeline.

\begin{honest}{The default database path writes into the repository, and one is committed}
\code{DEFAULT\_DB} is the bare relative path \code{agentrun.db}, so every command run
from the project root creates or extends \file{agentrun.db} \emph{in the working tree}.
\file{.gitignore} does not list it. Consequently \file{agentrun.db} is tracked in git:
36 KB holding 45 events across 3 run ids, one real run and two of its replays, exactly
matching the demo transcript in \file{docs/screenshots/run-output.txt}.

Following the README's own quickstart therefore dirties the repository, and \code{git
status} reports a modified binary the user did not knowingly touch. The stored database
is also not inert documentation: it contains a run whose \code{meta} points at an
absolute workspace path from the author's machine.

Nothing here is dangerous, and the file is small. But an event store is generated
output, and the fix is two lines: add \code{agentrun.db} to \file{.gitignore}, remove
it from the index, and default \code{AGENTRUN\_DB} somewhere outside the tree. If the
committed database is meant as a demo fixture, that intent should be stated and it
should be named like one.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{The web UI}
% ============================================================

\file{web/app.py} builds a small \textbf{FastAPI} application (a Python web framework;
here used to serve plain HTML rather than a JSON API) with three routes:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \code{GET /}: a table of every run: id, agent, status badge, event count, cost.
  \item \code{GET /runs/\{run\_id\}}: one run's status, cost and latency summary, any
  pending approvals rendered as a diff-style preview, and the full event timeline.
  \item \code{POST /runs/\{run\_id\}/calls/\{call\_id\}/approve} and \code{/deny}:
  record the decision, then call \code{resume\_run()} \emph{synchronously inside the
  request handler} before redirecting back.
\end{itemize}

All HTML is built by string concatenation with \code{html.escape()} applied to every
interpolated value, including the model's own text. That matters more than it looks:
the model's output is untrusted input, and it lands on a page an operator reads. The
escaping is applied consistently in the routes reviewed.

\subsection{Diff-style previews}

\code{web/preview.py}'s \code{preview(tool, args, workspace)} renders what a
\emph{pending} call would change, before a human approves it. For \code{write\_file} and
\code{csv\_update} it computes a real unified diff between the current file content and
what the call would produce, by simulating the operation in memory. For
\code{draft\_email} and \code{calendar\_add}, which have no natural ``before'' state, it
renders a \code{+}-prefixed summary. Anything else falls back to pretty-printed JSON
arguments.

\begin{jargon}{Unified diff}
A compact standard format for showing the difference between two versions of a text
file: unchanged lines plain, removed lines prefixed \code{-}, added lines prefixed
\code{+}. What \code{git diff} prints.
\end{jargon}

This is the single most product-minded idea in the repository. An approval gate that
shows an operator a blob of JSON arguments will be rubber-stamped within a day; one that
shows the actual diff of the actual file is a control someone might really read.

\begin{honest}{The preview is a re-implementation of the tool, and can disagree with it}
\code{preview.py} does not call the tool. It re-implements the tool's logic to predict
the outcome: its own CSV load, its own \code{append\_row} and \code{set\_cell}. Two
implementations of one behavior, with no test asserting they agree.

They already differ. \code{\_csv\_update} in \file{builtin.py} raises \code{ToolError}
for an out-of-range cell; \code{preview.py}'s \code{set\_cell} branch checks
\code{if r < len(new\_rows) and c < len(new\_rows[r])} and, when false, silently changes
nothing. So a proposed out-of-range \code{csv\_update} previews as ``(no textual
change)'', looks harmless, gets approved, and then fails. That is a benign direction to
fail in. The dangerous direction is the same structure with the roles reversed: any
future divergence where the preview under-states what the tool will do is a gate showing
the operator something other than what they are approving. The fix is to have the tools
expose a pure ``compute the new content'' function that both the handler and the preview
call.
\end{honest}

\begin{honest}{The gate has no authentication, and resumes inside the request handler}
Two issues, one from the README and one not.

The README's own: ``Approvals via the web UI resume the run synchronously inside the
request handler. A slow tool blocks the HTTP worker for its whole timeout. A real
deployment needs a worker process consuming a resume queue, with the UI only appending
decision events.'' Correct, and Section 8's finding sharpens it: a \emph{hung} tool does
not merely block for its timeout, it blocks until the handler returns.

Not in the README: there is no authentication of any kind. No login, no token, no CSRF
protection on the approve and deny POSTs. Anyone who can reach the port can approve any
side effect, and any page an operator visits can forge a cross-site POST to it. The
default bind is \code{127.0.0.1}, which is the mitigation and a real one, but
\code{--host} is offered with no warning attached. For most CRUD apps this would be
ordinary prototype scope. Here the approval gate \emph{is} the security control the
project is built around, so ``the control has no access control'' is worth stating
plainly rather than leaving for someone else to notice.
\end{honest}

% ============================================================
\section{Bootstrap, resume, and a real off-by-one}
% ============================================================

\file{bootstrap.py} exists so that ``build me a working \code{Runtime} for this run'' is
written once and shared by the CLI and the web UI. Its key idea, from its own docstring:
run metadata (workspace path, adapter kind, scenario file, behavior version) lives in the
\code{run\_created} event, so any process with the database can rebuild the exact runtime
needed to continue a run. No session state has to survive a process restart.

\code{resume\_run(store, run\_id, approval\_policy)}:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Project the log; if the run is terminal, return immediately.
  \item Count \code{model\_responded} events that are \emph{not} malformed. This is
  taken to be how many script turns the mock adapter consumed, and \code{script\_i} is
  repositioned to it.
  \item Separately count every \code{model\_responded} \emph{or} \code{model\_error}
  event. This is how many times \code{complete()} was called in total, and repositions
  \code{calls} so that fault matching on \code{at\_call} stays aligned.
  \item Call \code{drive(run\_id)}, which picks up where the fixed point left off.
\end{enumerate}

Step 3 is the fix the README describes. Step 2 is wrong.

\begin{honest}{Verified bug: resume rewinds the script by one after a scripted malformed turn}
The README concedes a nearby issue: ``a run resumed in the middle of an injected
model-fault sequence could realign fault triggers off by one.'' The actual defect is a
different one, in the other counter, and it is demonstrable.

Read \code{MockModelAdapter.complete()} carefully. The script index advances
\emph{before} the turn's content is inspected:

\begin{lstlisting}[language=Python]
step = self.script[self.script_i]
self.script_i += 1              # <- advances here, unconditionally
input_text = json.dumps(messages, sort_keys=True, default=str)

if "malformed" in step:
    raise MalformedOutputError("scripted malformed output", raw=step["malformed"])
\end{lstlisting}

So a \emph{scripted} \code{malformed:} turn does consume a script slot. (An
\emph{injected} malformed fault does not, because the fault plan is consulted earlier and
raises before this block. That asymmetry is deliberate and correct.) But that scripted
turn is recorded as \code{model\_responded} with \code{malformed: true}, and
\code{resume\_run}'s step 2 counts only \code{model\_responded} events that are
\textbf{not} malformed. It therefore does not count it.

Result: resuming a run whose script contained a scripted malformed turn rewinds
\code{script\_i} by one per such turn, and the malformed turn is served to the model
again. Confirmed by driving the adapter directly:

\begin{lstlisting}
scripted malformed: calls=1 script_i=1   -> consumed a script turn: True
injected malformed: calls=1 script_i=0   -> consumed a script turn: False

resume_run would set script_i = 0
the true position after that turn is  = 1
MISMATCH: resume rewinds the script by 1 turn; the malformed turn is served again.
\end{lstlisting}

Two shipped scenarios contain scripted malformed turns:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \file{scenarios/data\_entry/malformed\_then\_recover.yaml}
  \item \file{scenarios/research/malformed\_output\_fails\_run.yaml}
\end{itemize}

Neither is ever resumed by the test suite, and the scenario runner drives runs start to
finish in one process, so nothing exercises the combination. That is why 93 tests pass
over a real bug: the bug lives precisely in the gap between two features that are each
tested alone.

Consequence if hit: the resumed run re-raises the malformed output, burns a second entry
against \code{max\_malformed} (default 2), and can fail a run with
\code{malformed\_model\_output} that would have completed had it never been interrupted.
The fix the README already proposes is the right one and fixes both this and the issue it
does concede: record the adapter cursor in the \code{checkpoint} payload instead of
inferring it. The cursor is the one piece of state that is genuinely not a function of the
log, which is exactly why inferring it is where the event-sourced design springs a leak.
\end{honest}

\begin{keyidea}{The honest generalization, and the best thing to say about it in an interview}
The project's thesis is ``all state is a function of the log''. This bug is what happens
at the one place where that is not quite true.

The mock adapter's script position is real state living outside the log, and
\code{resume\_run} tries to reconstruct it by inference. The inference is a heuristic
(``one script turn per non-malformed response'') that happens to be wrong for one case.
Every other feature in this project is exact because it reads the log; this one is
approximate because it guesses at something the log does not record.

That is not an argument against event sourcing. It is the strongest argument \emph{for}
it in the whole codebase: the single component that opted out of the discipline is the
single component with a latent bug. And it is confined to the mock adapter, which is
test infrastructure, so no production path is affected.
\end{keyidea}

% ============================================================
\section{Package map}
% ============================================================

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{forest} dirtree
[src/agent\_runtime/
  [events.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ event types + canonical JSON encoding}]
  [store.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ SQLite append-only store + idempotency ledger}]
  [projection.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ log to RunState, log to messages}]
  [runtime.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ the loop, approval gating}]
  [replay.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ recorded stand-ins + comparator}]
  [faults.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ fault plans}]
  [accounting.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ usage to cost/latency}]
  [agents.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ example agent definitions}]
  [bootstrap.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ build runtimes for new/existing runs}]
  [cli.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ agentrun entry point}]
  [adapters/
    [base.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ ModelAdapter interface, ModelTurn, Usage}]
    [mock.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ scripted offline adapter}]
    [anthropic\_adapter.py]
    [openai\_adapter.py]
  ]
  [tools/
    [registry.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ ToolSpec, ToolRegistry, sandbox resolve}]
    [executor.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ timeout, retries, idempotency}]
    [builtin.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ the 8 shipped tools}]
  ]
  [scenarios/
    [loader.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ YAML schema + loading}]
    [runner.py \textnormal{\scriptsize\itshape\ \ run one scenario, check assertions}]
